title: Driven two-level systems as a minimal resource for remote entanglement stabilization
authors: Philippe Gigon, Adrian Parra-Rodriguez, Joan Agustí, Peter Rabl
abstract: We analyze the autonomous stabilization of remote entanglement by driving two distant qubits with the output of a correlated photon source. By treating the qubits as idealized entanglement detectors, we develop a general framework to quantify the maximum amount of entanglement that can be remotely stabilized in this way with a given photon source. We then apply this approach to evaluate the suitability of a single driven two-level system as a minimal resource for autonomous entanglement distribution schemes. While our analysis confirms the presence of distributable entanglement in the Mollow sidebands of a bare two-level system, we show that stabilizing close to maximally entangled states requires additional filter cavities that enhance the relevant correlated emission events compared to other processes. We identify optimized driving and cavity parameters and explain the achievable amount of entanglement in different regimes in terms of an effective two-mode squeezing model. These insights are particularly relevant for quantum networks based on photons or phonons in solid-state systems, where isolated spins, impurity centers, or other two-level defects are readily available, while alternative sources of correlated photons are difficult to realize.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework to quantify how entanglement can be remotely stabilized between two qubits using a correlated photon source derived from a driven two-level system. The authors demonstrate that while the basic setup yields some entanglement, optimizing the system by adding filter cavities is crucial to approach the theoretical maximum. This has direct implications for building quantum networks using solid-state defects.
main_problem: The paper analyzes the autonomous stabilization of remote entanglement between two distant qubits using the correlated photon emission from a driven two-level system (TLS) source.
main_result: While a bare TLS shows some entanglement, achieving near-maximal entanglement requires coupling the TLS to additional filter cavities to enhance specific correlated emission events.
method: The analysis develops a general framework using master equations and two-time correlation functions to quantify distributable entanglement, comparing different physical regimes (e.g., Purcell vs. Qubit-mediated squeezing).
model_or_system: The system involves two target qubits coupled to a correlated photon source, which is modeled as a driven TLS interacting with optical cavities. The dynamics are governed by a master equation incorporating source, qubit, and interaction terms.
key_observables: Distributable entanglement (quantified by concurrence, $C_d$), effective two-mode squeezing parameters ($	ext{reff}, \mu_{	ext{eff}}$), and the steady-state density matrix.
important_parameters: TLS drive strength ($\Omega_0$), cavity decay rate ($\kappa$), qubit detunings ($\Delta_q$), and the ratio of decay rates ($\gamma/\kappa$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA) and various adiabatic elimination techniques depending on the coupling regime (e.g., Purcell or QMS).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematics of the quantum network, plot the achievable concurrence ($C_d$) versus driving parameters ($\Omega_0, \Delta_0$), and show the dependence of entanglement on the ratio of decay rates ($\gamma/\kappa$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from a general framework for entanglement quantification to specific applications using a bare TLS, then systematically explores enhancements by coupling the TLS to structured cavities, analyzing different physical regimes (Purcell, QMS) to maximize entanglement.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed theoretical roadmap for engineering entanglement distribution in solid-state quantum networks, showing how cavity engineering can overcome intrinsic limitations of simple sources.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematics of the basic chiral quantum network considered in this work. Here, a source (black box) produces a stream of correlated photons that drive two distant target qubits into an entangled steady state. See text for more details.

Figure 2: Operative definition of distributable entanglement. In the Markovian regime (κi ≫γi), the target qubits see an effective TMS environment, which is fully characterized by the effective squeezing strength reff and effective purity µeff. These parameters, in turn, can be evaluated from a set of two-time correlation functions of the CPS, see Eq. (9) and Eq. (12). For a given reff and µeff the concurrence Cd ≡C(reff, µeff) of the resulting qubit steady state is given in Eq. (13) and plotted in the lower left corner. The white dashed line marks the minimal conditions above which Cd > 0. Overall, this approach allows us to characterize the CPS in terms of a single, physically intuitive...

Figure 3: (a) Schematics of a CPS consisting of a single TLS. The TLS is driven by an external field of frequency ωd and decays into the two waveguides with rates κ and into an independent environment with rate Γ0. (b) Plot of the distributable entanglement as a function of the drive strength, Ω0, and the detuning of the drive, ∆0, and for target qubits tuned in resonance with the Mollow sidebands (∆q,1 = −∆q,2 = ˜Ω). The two symbols indicate the weak- driving (cross) and strong-driving (star) regimes. For both regimes, the dependence of the two relevant correlation func- tions Re{C11 −+(∆q)} ∼N1 and |C12 −−(∆q)| ∼|M| on the detuning ∆q is sketched in the respective plots in (c) and (d)....

Figure 4: Plots of (a) the effective squeezing strength and (b) the effective purity for a driven TLS and for the same parameters as in Fig. 3(b). The opposite trend of those two quantities results in optimal entanglement for ∆0 ≈Ω0.

Figure 5: (a) The CPS consists of a driven, dissipative TLS coherently coupled to two bosonic modes a1 and a2 with strength g. We assume that the modes selectively emit into one or the other waveguide, that is O1 = a1, O2 = a2. The TLS has the same parameters as shown in Fig. 3(a). (b) In the resolved-Purcell regime, ˜Ω≫κ ≫g, two cavities can be adiabatically eliminated to obtain an effective model for the TLS, with jump operators O1 = ˜τ −, O2 = ˜τ + and decay rates ˜κ1 and ˜κ2. (c) In the opposite regime, where Γ0 ≫g, κ, one can adiabatically eliminate the TLS, and model the re- maining dynamics of the two cavities by a quadratic TMS Lindbladian.
